3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.

124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.

126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.

132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.

136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).

145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G := G.\mathrm{rightDerived}\,n : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) for
33	the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\) (the Mathlib anchor
36	\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is left
37	exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(anchor \ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Mathlib dependency anchors}
42	
43	The development stands on three results that Mathlib already provides for the
44	injective-resolution construction of \(R^n G\). They are recorded here as
45	first-class blocks so that the route's reliance on Mathlib is explicit and the
46	dependency graph resolves.
47	
48	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
49	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
50	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
51	  \mathlibok
52	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
53	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
54	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
55	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
56	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each

58	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)

67	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.

129	\begin{definition}[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}

137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.

154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]

164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.

173	  of ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\)'' is ``\(\mathrm{IsZero}\,((R^k G).\mathrm{obj}\,J)\)''
174	  using the \texttt{Limits.IsZero} predicate rather than an equality to a chosen
175	  zero object. Two equivalent quantifier shapes are available: ``for all \(k \geq
176	  1\)'' (a hypothesis \(1 \le k\)) or the index-shifted ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J)\) is zero
177	  for all \(k\)''; the latter avoids the inequality side-condition and matches
178	  Mathlib's \texttt{isZero\_rightDerived\_obj\_injective\_succ}
179	  (anchor~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), so it is the recommended
180	  definitional form.
181	\end{definition}

183	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
184	
185	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
186	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
187	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
188	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
189	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is
190	the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the route requires; Mathlib
191	provides the building blocks (\(\mathrm{InjectiveResolution}\) of an object via enough
192	injectives) but does not appear to ship the lift itself, so it is recorded here as an
193	explicit project obligation.
194	
195	\begin{lemma}[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
196	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
197	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
198	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
199	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
200	  \[
201	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
202	  \]
203	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
204	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
205	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
206	  \[
207	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
208	  \]
209	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
210	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
211	  \[
212	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
213	      \longrightarrow 0
214	  \]
215	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
216	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
217	\end{lemma}
218	
219	\begin{proof}
220	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
221	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma; see e.g.\ Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
222	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8 (stated there for projective resolutions; the
223	  injective-resolution form is the dual). Choose injective resolutions
224	  \(A \to I_A^\bullet\) and \(C \to I_C^\bullet\), which exist because \(\mathcal{A}\)
225	  has enough injectives. Set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) in each degree; this is
226	  injective, being a finite direct sum of injectives. The maps
227	  \(I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and \(I_B^n \to I_C^n\) are the canonical coproduct inclusion
228	  and product projection, so each degree is split exact by construction. It remains
229	  to build the augmentation \(B \to I_B^0\) and the differentials
230	  \(I_B^n \to I_B^{n+1}\) making \(I_B^\bullet\) a resolution of \(B\) and the two
231	  vertical families chain maps. One proceeds degree by degree: at each stage the
232	  required lift exists because the relevant target summand \(I_A^{\bullet}\) is
233	  injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism, so the universal lifting
234	  property of injectives produces the missing component; the diagonal differential of
235	  \(I_B^\bullet\) is then assembled from the differentials of \(I_A^\bullet\),
236	  \(I_C^\bullet\) and these lifts. Exactness of the resulting \(I_B^\bullet\) (that it
237	  resolves \(B\)) follows from the long exact homology sequence
238	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to the degreewise-split short
239	  exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet \to 0\): the
240	  outer complexes are acyclic resolutions, so the connecting maps force
241	  \(I_B^\bullet\) to be acyclic in positive degrees and to have \(H^0 = B\).
242	\end{proof}

244	\section{The dimension-shift step}
245	
246	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
247	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
248	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
249	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
250	complex-level homology long exact sequence
251	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
252	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
253	sub-lemma below.
254	
255	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
256	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
257	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
258	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
259	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
260	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
261	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
262	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
263	  % between abelian categories and assume
264	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
265	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
266	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
267	  % \begin{enumerate}
268	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
269	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
270	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
271	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
272	  % \end{enumerate}
273	  % In each of the three cases
274	  % $$
275	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
276	  % $$
277	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
278	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
279	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
280	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
281	  \[
282	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
283	  \]
284	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
285	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
286	  \begin{enumerate}
287	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
288	      furnish isomorphisms
289	      \[
290	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
291	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
292	      \]
293	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
294	      \[
295	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
296	      \]
297	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
298	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
299	  \end{enumerate}
300	\end{lemma}
301	
302	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
303	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the bounded-below derived category
304	% $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$. Mathlib provides no such delta-functor at the level of the
305	% right-derived-functor OBJECTS $(R^k G)$, so the proof below is project-original:
306	% it constructs the same long exact sequence from the complex-level homology long
307	% exact sequence (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe
308	% lift (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). No external verbatim proof
309	% quote is attached, as the route differs from the cited source's.
310	\begin{proof}
311	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
312	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
313	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
314	  We first manufacture the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
315	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\); this is the step that, in the classical
316	  treatment, is supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the
317	  derived category, but which we must instead build by hand from the complex-level
318	  homology long exact sequence.
319	
320	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
321	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
322	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
323	  complexes
324	  \[
325	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
326	  \]
327	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
328	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
329	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
330	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
331	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
332	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
333	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
334	  \[
335	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
336	      \longrightarrow 0
337	  \]
338	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
339	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
340	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
341	  \[
342	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
343	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
344	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
345	  \]
346	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
347	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
348	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
349	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
350	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
351	  sequence of right-derived functors
352	  \[
353	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
354	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
355	  \]
356	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
357	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
358	  \[
359	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
360	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
361	  \]
362	
363	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
364	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
365	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
366	  \[
367	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
368	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
369	  \]
370	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
371	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
372	  In the lowest degree the segment
373	  \[
374	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
375	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
376	  \]
377	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
378	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
379	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
380	  part (2).
381	\end{proof}

459	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, with the long exact sequence at each
460	  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
461	  resolution into the
462	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
463	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
464	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
465	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
466	  \[
467	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
468	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
469	  \]
470	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
471	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.
472	
473	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
474	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
475	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
476	  \[
477	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
478	      = (R^0 G)(A),
479	  \]
480	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
481	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
482	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
483	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
484	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
485	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
486	  \[
487	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
488	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
489	  \]
490	  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
491	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
492	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
493	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
494	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
495	
496	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
497	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
498	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
499	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
500	  \[
501	    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
502	  \]
503	  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
504	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
505	  isomorphisms down the staircase
506	  \[
507	    (R^n G)(A)
508	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
509	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
510	      \;\cong\; \cdots
511	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
512	  \]
513	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
514	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
515	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
516	  \[
517	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
518	  \]
519	  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
520	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
521	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
522	  \[
523	    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
524	      \;\cong\;
525	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
526	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
527	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
528	  \]
529	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
530	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
531	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
532	
533	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel
534	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
535	  horseshoe lift (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) and the complex-level
536	  homology long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) --- on
537	  the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\)
538	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). The injective objects supplied
539	  by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
540	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
541	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
542	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
543	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
544	\end{proof}

312	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
313	  complexes
314	  \[
315	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
316	  \]

326	      \longrightarrow 0
327	  \]

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
98	  \[
99	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
100	      \longrightarrow 0
101	  \]
102	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
103	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
104	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
105	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
106	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
107	  \[
108	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
109	  \]
110	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
111	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
112	  \[
113	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
114	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
115	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
116	  \]
117	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
118	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
119	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
120	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
124	
125	\begin{definition}[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
126	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
127	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
128	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
129	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
130	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
131	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
132	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
133	  % L5594--5617).
134	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
135	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
136	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
137	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
138	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
139	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
140	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
141	  % between abelian categories and assume
142	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
143	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
144	  % \begin{enumerate}
145	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
146	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
147	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
148	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
149	  % \end{enumerate}"
150	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
151	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
152	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
153	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
154	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
155	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
156	  \[
157	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
158	  \]
159	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
160	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
161	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
162	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
163	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
164	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
165	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
166	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
167	
168	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
169	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
170	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
171	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
172	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
176	
177	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
178	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
179	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
180	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
181	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
182	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
183	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
184	lift.
185	
186	\begin{lemma}[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
187	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
188	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
189	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
190	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
191	  \[
192	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
193	  \]
194	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
195	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
196	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
197	  \[
198	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
199	  \]
200	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
201	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
202	  \[
203	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
204	      \longrightarrow 0
205	  \]
206	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
207	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
208	\end{lemma}
209	
210	\begin{proof}
211	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
212	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma; see e.g.\ Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
213	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8 (stated there for projective resolutions; the
214	  injective-resolution form is the dual). Choose injective resolutions
215	  \(A \to I_A^\bullet\) and \(C \to I_C^\bullet\), which exist because \(\mathcal{A}\)
216	  has enough injectives. Set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) in each degree; this is
217	  injective, being a finite direct sum of injectives. The maps
218	  \(I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and \(I_B^n \to I_C^n\) are the canonical coproduct inclusion
219	  and product projection, so each degree is split exact by construction. It remains
220	  to build the augmentation \(B \to I_B^0\) and the differentials
221	  \(I_B^n \to I_B^{n+1}\) making \(I_B^\bullet\) a resolution of \(B\) and the two
222	  vertical families chain maps. One proceeds degree by degree: at each stage the
223	  required lift exists because the relevant target summand \(I_A^{\bullet}\) is
224	  injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism, so the universal lifting
225	  property of injectives produces the missing component; the diagonal differential of
226	  \(I_B^\bullet\) is then assembled from the differentials of \(I_A^\bullet\),
227	  \(I_C^\bullet\) and these lifts. Exactness of the resulting \(I_B^\bullet\) (that it
228	  resolves \(B\)) follows from the long exact homology sequence
229	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to the degreewise-split short
230	  exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet \to 0\): the
231	  outer complexes are acyclic resolutions, so the connecting maps force
232	  \(I_B^\bullet\) to be acyclic in positive degrees and to have \(H^0 = B\).
233	\end{proof}
234	
235	\section{The dimension-shift step}
236	
237	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
238	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
239	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
240	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
241	complex-level homology long exact sequence
242	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
243	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
244	sub-lemma below.
245	
246	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
247	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
248	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
249	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
250	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
251	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
252	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
253	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
254	  % between abelian categories and assume
255	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
256	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
257	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
258	  % \begin{enumerate}
259	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
260	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
261	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
262	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
263	  % \end{enumerate}
264	  % In each of the three cases
265	  % $$
266	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
267	  % $$
268	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
269	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
270	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
271	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
272	  \[
273	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
274	  \]
275	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
276	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
277	  \begin{enumerate}
278	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
279	      furnish isomorphisms
280	      \[
281	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
282	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
283	      \]
284	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
285	      \[
286	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
287	      \]
288	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
289	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
290	  \end{enumerate}
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
294	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
295	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
296	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
297	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
298	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
299	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
300	\begin{proof}
301	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
302	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
303	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
304	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
305	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
306	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
307	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
308	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
309	
310	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
311	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
312	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
313	  complexes
314	  \[
315	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
316	  \]
317	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
318	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
319	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
320	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
321	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
322	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
323	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
324	  \[
325	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
326	      \longrightarrow 0
327	  \]
328	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
329	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
330	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
331	  \[
332	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
333	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
334	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
335	  \]
336	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
337	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
338	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
339	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
340	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
341	  sequence of right-derived functors
342	  \[
343	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
344	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
345	  \]
346	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
347	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
348	  \[
349	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
350	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
351	  \]
352	
353	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
354	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
355	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
356	  \[
357	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
358	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
359	  \]
360	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
361	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
362	  In the lowest degree the segment
363	  \[
364	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
365	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
366	  \]
367	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
368	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
369	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
370	  part (2).
371	\end{proof}
372	
373	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
374	
375	\begin{theorem}[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
376	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}

399	  % and we obtain an exact functor
400	  % $$
401	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
402	  % $$
403	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
404	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
405	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
406	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
407	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
408	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
409	  Let
410	  \[
411	    0 \longrightarrow A
412	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
413	      \longrightarrow \cdots
414	  \]
415	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
416	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
417	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
418	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
419	  \[
420	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
421	  \]
422	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
423	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
424	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
425	  object.
426	\end{theorem}
427	
428	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
429	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
430	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
431	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
432	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
433	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
434	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
435	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
436	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
437	% the long exact cohomology sequence
438	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
439	% to an exact sequence
440	% $$

442	% $$

450	  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
451	  resolution into the
452	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
453	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
454	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
455	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
456	  \[
457	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
458	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
459	  \]
460	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
461	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.

463	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
464	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
465	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
466	  \[
467	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
468	      = (R^0 G)(A),
469	  \]
470	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
471	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
472	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
473	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
474	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
475	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
476	  \[
477	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
478	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
479	  \]
480	  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
481	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
482	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
483	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
484	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
485	
486	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
487	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
488	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
489	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
490	  \[
491	    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
492	  \]
493	  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
494	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
495	  isomorphisms down the staircase

498	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
499	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
500	      \;\cong\; \cdots
501	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
502	  \]
503	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
504	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
505	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
506	  \[
507	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
508	  \]
509	  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
510	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
511	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
512	  \[

514	      \;\cong\;
515	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
516	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
517	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
518	  \]
519	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
520	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
521	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).

523	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel

532	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
533	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
534	\end{proof}
